reasoning_summary: The live run 20260421_084952_bf16_gemm_v1_8b1af08 measured 24.427104 ms, and the current source blob is already identical to the previously best-measured 489574e PTX surface, so a pure restore is no longer a meaningful next move. This run is still slower than the best measured result while NCU shows tensor activity below 50 percent, active warps at 16.61 percent, and non-trivial long-scoreboard plus barrier stalls. That points to control-path and address-generation overhead inside the active 128x128 hot-band kernels rather than a missing macro-tile change. I ranked a low-risk steady-state shared-offset hoist first, a tighter PTX export-address cleanup second, and a riskier wait-group/barrier handoff retime third.
family_audit: reject exact restore as the primary move because HEAD, 8b1af08, and 489574e already share the same kernel blob; another replay would only duplicate the old helper failure mode.
accept low-risk hot-band control-path tightening in the active 128x128 PTX and non-PTX kernels because the run still shows low tensor utilization with room to trim integer/shared address work.
defer large geometry changes such as the 256x128 pivot branch because they have much higher regression risk than the current performance gap justifies.

directions.name: Hoist 128x128 Hot-Band Shared Offsets Out Of The Steady-State Loop
directions.hypothesis: Both active 128x128 hot-band kernels recompute warp-local A-row and B-column shared offsets inside the K steady-state loop even though those offsets are invariant for the entire CTA. Hoisting those offsets once per warp should shave integer address work and reduce control dilution on a path where tensor activity is only 48.37 percent while DRAM is far from saturated.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Warp-local shared-pointer arithmetic and loop-carried control overhead in the 128x128 hot-band steady state are stealing issue slots from tensor work.
directions.risk: Low. The change is local, preserves the same tile geometry, and only hoists invariant offset math.
ranking_notes: This is the cleanest way to test whether the current gap versus the best measured run is coming from hot-loop control overhead instead of macro geometry.
It touches both active 128x128 hot-band variants without reopening the old restore-or-replay path.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the implementation needs to change CTA geometry, cp.async stage count, or the dispatch map instead of remaining a local offset-hoist cleanup.

directions.name: Trim PTX 64x64 Export Address Math In The Hot-Band Epilogue
directions.hypothesis: The PTX 64x64 export microkernel repeatedly rebuilds tile-column and row addresses while draining each accumulator tile from shared scratch. Precomputing the row base and tile-column base once could shorten the epilogue integer path and slightly reduce the barrier-protected export window.
directions.expected_bottleneck: PTX export-side address generation in the 64x64 writer is adding integer/control overhead after the MMA loop.
directions.risk: Low to moderate. The code motion is local but it sits inside the final store path, so a pointer mistake would break correctness immediately.
ranking_notes: This is a plausible follow-up if the steady-state offset hoist is flat, because the PTX export path is still one of the few hot regions with repeated address math.
I ranked it behind dir_01 because the active NCU signature still looks more like steady-state under-utilization than pure epilogue pain.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the cleanup starts changing export ordering or scratch layout rather than only precomputing existing addresses.

directions.name: Retime The 128x128 PTX Wait-Group And Consumer Barrier Handoff
directions.hypothesis: Barrier stalls are not dominant but still non-trivial at 5.33 percent, and the active PTX kernels fence the consumer path in a conservative wait-group then __syncthreads pattern. A careful retime could overlap the future prefetch slightly earlier and cut some of that handoff cost.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The PTX 128x128 steady state may be overserializing cp.async wait-group completion and CTA-wide consumer handoff.
directions.risk: Moderate to high. The schedule is correctness-sensitive and easy to destabilize if the producer-consumer handoff moves too early.
ranking_notes: The upside may be larger than dir_01 or dir_02, but the correctness and regression risk are materially higher because the cp.async handoff is fragile.
I kept it third so this round stays on a bounded low-risk implementation.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the retime requires changing stage count, group depth, or grouped-row topology instead of a local handoff reorder.

notes: The current kernel already matches the best historical PTX grouping surface at the source level, so this diagnosis avoids another replay cycle and focuses on real low-risk control-path tightening in the active 128x128 hot-band kernels.
